blocks.statement: I can decompose a projectile's motion into independent horizontal and vertical pieces, and I can explain why a side-pushed ball and a dropped ball hit the ground at the same time.

blocks.whatWeKnow: An asteroid in flight is technically a projectile — gravity bends its path.
blocks.connection: What you learn about projectiles in 3 days scales straight up to orbits. The math is the same; only the distances grow.

blocks.markdown:
## Two motions, one object

A projectile's motion splits into two **independent** pieces:

- **Horizontal:** x = vₓ · t — horizontal velocity **never changes** (no horizontal force).
- **Vertical:** y = y₀ − ½·g·t² — vertical velocity changes at g per second (gravity acts here).

The two pieces don't talk to each other. **Time is the only link** between them.

terms.definition: Any object moving under gravity alone after launch — no engine, no air force. A thrown baseball. A horizontally-fired bullet. An asteroid in free fall.
terms.cognate: Sp. proyectil · Pt. projétil · HC pwojektil

blocks.title: The drop-and-push race
blocks.markdown: Drop one ball. Push another sideways at the **same instant**. They hit the ground at the **SAME time**. Time is set by the VERTICAL motion alone — the sideways push changes *where* it lands, not *when*.

blocks.title: In class today — monkey and hunter
blocks.markdown: We watched the classic monkey-and-hunter setup: aim straight at the target, and if the target drops the instant you fire, you ALWAYS hit it — both fall at the same g. Run the simulation below and test which prediction survives.

blocks.instruction: Draw the dropped ball and the side-pushed ball at three moments (t = 0, 1 s, 2 s). For the pushed ball, label vₓ at each moment (it never changes) and v_y at each moment (it grows by ~10 m/s each second).
prompts: Both balls at the SAME height at each moment.
vₓ labels: same value all three times.

blocks.prompt: Why does a side-pushed ball NOT take longer to fall than a ball that's just dropped? Use the word 'independent' in your answer.
blocks.frame: The horizontal and vertical motions are ___, so the sideways push changes ___ but not ___.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **5.1–5.4** (vectors and scalars, velocity vectors, components of vectors, and what makes a projectile). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
